name: Email Abuse Patterns Library
model: Email Abuse Intelligence Reference Model v1.0
description: Defensive campaign archetypes composed from Email Abuse Intelligence techniques. Analytical compositions for classification and defence; not attributed to any actor and containing no operational detail.

patterns.name: Exact-domain spoofing of an unauthenticated domain
patterns.summary: The simplest, most preventable abuse chain: because a domain never published enforcing SPF/DMARC, anyone can send mail as it — and receivers have nothing to contradict them.

chain.name: Missing-SPF exploitation
chain.note: No SPF record — no server list to contradict a forgery.

chain.name: DMARC-none exploitation
chain.note: DMARC absent or at p=none — failures are reported, never rejected.

chain.name: Header-from spoofing
chain.note: The visible From is set to the target domain exactly.

chain.name: Display-name impersonation
chain.note: Optionally the display name alone is impersonated for mobile clients.

chain.name: Phishing enablement
chain.note: The trusted envelope now carries a phishing lure…

chain.name: BEC enablement
chain.note: …or a business-email-compromise instruction.

breakpoint.text: This is the one chain the SpamCrackers domain check measures directly. Publishing SPF with -all and DMARC at p=reject with alignment removes the opportunity at its root — the forgery is rejected before delivery.

signals: From = your domain, sent from a server not in any SPF include.
DMARC aggregate reports show unauthenticated sources under your domain.
SPF result none/softfail and DMARC policy none in the received headers.

patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-middle credential phishing
patterns.summary: A phishing chain built to defeat multi-factor authentication by relaying the login in real time and stealing the authenticated session itself.

chain.name: OSINT profiling
chain.note: Public profiling identifies the person and the service they use.

chain.name: Lookalike / cousin domains
chain.note: A lookalike / cousin domain is registered to host the proxy.

chain.name: TLS-for-trust
chain.note: Valid TLS lends the padlock's false reassurance.

chain.name: Account-security pretext
chain.note: An account-security pretext manufactures urgency to sign in.

chain.name: Adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM)
chain.note: A reverse proxy relays the session to the genuine login in real time.

chain.name: OTP interception
chain.note: The one-time code is intercepted mid-flight.

chain.name: Account takeover
chain.note: The stolen session cookie yields account takeover.

chain.name: Mailbox persistence
chain.note: Mailbox rules are planted for quiet persistence.

breakpoint.text: Because AiTM defeats one-time codes, the durable break is phishing-resistant authentication (hardware-bound passkeys / FIDO2), which will not authenticate to a proxy origin. Upstream, monitoring for lookalike registrations shortens the window before the lure ever sends.

signals: Login origin is a proxy/lookalike host, not the real service domain.
A session cookie is used from a new geography minutes after sign-in.
New inbox rules that auto-delete or forward security notifications.

patterns.name: Vendor-compromise invoice redirection
patterns.summary: The highest-loss email fraud pattern: a genuine vendor mailbox is compromised and used, from inside a real thread, to redirect a legitimate payment.

chain.name: Relationship mapping
chain.note: Buyer–supplier relationships are mapped.

chain.name: Vendor email compromise (VEC)
chain.note: The vendor's own mailbox is compromised (VEC).

chain.name: Thread-context harvesting
chain.note: Live threads are read for context, tone and timing.

chain.name: Pending-payment discovery
chain.note: A pending, unpaid invoice is identified.

chain.name: Thread insertion
chain.note: The message is inserted into the genuine thread.

chain.name: Vendor bank-change request
chain.note: A bank-detail change is requested as routine.

chain.name: Banking-detail alteration
chain.note: Payment routing is altered to a controlled account.

chain.name: Wire-transfer instruction
chain.note: The wire executes to the new details.

chain.name: Money-mule routing
chain.note: Funds are moved through money-mule accounts.

breakpoint.text: Authentication can't catch this — the mail is genuinely the vendor's. The break is procedural: any change to banking details is confirmed on a previously-known phone number, never a number supplied in the email. That single control neutralises the entire chain regardless of how convincing the thread is.

signals: Banking details change partway through an existing thread.
A new reply-to or subtle send-time / language shift from the vendor.
Pressure to pay before a stated deadline, discouraging a call-back.

patterns.name: Executive-impersonation gift-card fraud
patterns.summary: A low-tech, high-volume BEC chain: a junior employee is pressured by a spoofed executive into buying gift cards under a veil of confidentiality.

chain.name: Finance-role identification
chain.note: A junior finance/admin role is identified as the target.

chain.name: Display-name impersonation
chain.note: The executive's display name is impersonated.

chain.name: Executive request
chain.note: An urgent executive request is issued.

chain.name: Confidentiality framing
chain.note: Confidentiality framing isolates the victim from colleagues.

chain.name: Gift-card request
chain.note: Gift cards are requested and their codes exfiltrated.

chain.name: Verification suppression
chain.note: Verification is actively suppressed ('I'm in a meeting').

breakpoint.control: A 'no gift cards, always verify' norm
breakpoint.text: Enforcing DMARC and display-name / external-sender banners strips the disguise, and a standing rule — leadership never requests gift cards by email, and any such request is verified in person — makes the verification-suppression step fail. The chain depends on the victim not checking; remove that and it collapses.

signals: Executive display name over a free-webmail or lookalike address.
Urgency plus secrecy: 'don't tell anyone, I'm unavailable to call'.
A request for gift-card purchases or their redemption codes.

patterns.name: Macro-document loader to ransomware
patterns.summary: The classic malware-delivery chain: a lure document runs a macro that pulls a loader, which stages the final ransomware payload.

chain.name: Macro-enabled document
chain.note: A macro-enabled Office document is prepared as the lure.

chain.name: Attachment delivery
chain.note: It is delivered as an email attachment.

chain.name: Password gating
chain.note: Password gating hides the payload from gateway scanners.

chain.name: Sandbox evasion
chain.note: Sandbox checks delay execution in analysis environments.

chain.name: Macro execution
chain.note: Enabling content runs the macro.

chain.name: Dropper / loader stage
chain.note: A dropper / loader stage fetches the next payload.

chain.name: Autostart persistence
chain.note: Autostart entries establish persistence.

chain.name: Ransomware impact
chain.note: Ransomware encrypts and extorts.

breakpoint.control: Block macros from the internet; detonate attachments
breakpoint.text: Disabling macros in files that arrive from the internet by policy prevents the execution step outright. Detonating attachments in a sandbox and stripping active content at the gateway remove the delivery advantage the password-gating was designed to buy.

signals: A password-protected archive with the password written in the email body.
A document that insists you 'Enable Editing / Enable Content'.
Outbound fetch to an unfamiliar host immediately after opening a document.

patterns.name: Reply-chain hijack malware delivery
patterns.summary: Malware delivered from a real, compromised account into a genuine existing thread — trust is borrowed from the conversation, so authentication alone won't stop it.

chain.name: Compromised sender account
chain.note: A legitimate sender account is compromised.

chain.name: Reply-chain delivery
chain.note: The payload is sent as a reply within a real thread.

chain.name: HTML smuggling package
chain.note: HTML smuggling assembles the file in the browser to evade scanning.

chain.name: User-run persuasion
chain.note: Familiar context persuades the user to run it.

chain.name: Remote control
chain.note: The operator gains remote control of the host.

breakpoint.text: Because the sender is genuine and the thread is real, DMARC and reputation pass — so the break must be at the content: detonate attachments and reconstruct smuggled files server-side, and flag unexpected archives or HTML attachments even from known correspondents.

signals: A reply into a genuine old thread that suddenly carries an attachment.
An HTML attachment that assembles a downloadable file locally.
An archive or macro document unexpected from a known contact.

patterns.name: Snowshoe credential-harvest spam
patterns.summary: Volume abuse that spreads a campaign thinly across many low-reputation sources to stay under per-IP thresholds while funnelling victims to a credential-harvest page.

chain.name: Snowshoe distribution
chain.note: Snowshoe distribution spreads volume across many /24s.

chain.name: Reputation warming
chain.note: Reputation warming primes fresh sending identities.

chain.name: Urgency & scarcity framing
chain.note: Urgency & scarcity framing drives the click.

chain.name: Brand & authority impersonation
chain.note: Brand & authority impersonation borrows trust.

chain.name: Redirect & cloaking chains
chain.note: Redirect & cloaking chains hide the true destination.

chain.name: Credential-harvest destinations
chain.note: A credential-harvest destination collects logins.

chain.name: Credential & data resale
chain.note: Harvested credentials & data are resold.

breakpoint.text: Per-IP thresholds are exactly what snowshoe defeats, so the break is aggregate: cross-source reputation and campaign clustering catch the fan of thin senders, while unwrapping redirect chains exposes the shared harvest destination the lures all point to.

signals: Many low-reputation sources each sending tiny volumes of similar mail.
Freshly-registered or recently-warmed sending domains.
Links that pass through cloaking redirects to a lookalike login page.

patterns.name: Advance-fee & romance fraud
patterns.summary: A long-game social chain that builds rapport, moves the victim off-platform, then escalates a series of fees against a manufactured payoff that never arrives.

chain.name: Mass blast
chain.note: A mass blast seeds a wide pool of potential victims.

chain.name: Romance opener
chain.note: A romance (or windfall/investment) opener engages a responder.

chain.name: Small reciprocity
chain.note: Small reciprocity manufactures a sense of obligation.

chain.name: Channel migration
chain.note: The victim is migrated to a private channel, off oversight.

chain.name: Rising fees
chain.note: Rising fees are introduced one after another.

chain.name: Sunk-cost pressure
chain.note: Sunk-cost pressure keeps the victim paying.

chain.name: Advance-fee demand
chain.note: An advance-fee demand extracts the first payment…

chain.name: Crypto transfer
chain.note: …over irreversible crypto rails.

chain.name: Mule networks
chain.note: Mule networks launder the proceeds.

breakpoint.text: The reliable break is a rule, not a filter: a legitimate prize, employer or partner never requires an upfront fee or crypto payment to release money. Channel migration off a monitored platform is the earliest observable tell — treat it as the moment to disengage and verify.

signals: A quick push to move to WhatsApp/Telegram or private email.
Escalating fees, taxes or 'release charges' against a promised payoff.
Payment demanded in gift cards or cryptocurrency.
